required.title: [Wall Map] A large physical/political world map

suggested.title: [Read Aloud] How to Make an Apple Pie and See the World
suggested.author: Marjorie Priceman

suggested.title: [Read Aloud] Follow the Water from Brook to Ocean
suggested.author: Arthur Dorros

suggested.title: [Look Together] Google Earth or similar digital globe

supplementary.title: [For Mentor] Physical Geography: Great Systems and Global Environments
supplementary.author: William M. Marsh and Martin M. Kaufman

supplementary.title: [For Mentor] Annals of the Former World
supplementary.author: John McPhee

supplementary.title: [Read Aloud] The Magic School Bus Inside the Earth
supplementary.author: Joanna Cole

competence_markers.description: The student can identify and locate all seven continents and five oceans on a globe and a flat map without assistance
The student can locate and name at least five major rivers and five major mountain ranges on a map
The student can identify and name major landforms (mountains, valleys, plains, plateaus, islands, peninsulas) and explain how at least two of them form
The student can explain that the Earth's surface moves and changes, and give an example (plate tectonics, mountain building, erosion)
The student can identify major deserts and understands that deserts are barriers to movement and settlement
The student demonstrates cardinal direction competence — knows north, south, east, west and can orient on a map
The student understands that maps are representations that involve distortion, and can describe at least one difference between a globe and a flat map
The student can read a basic map — using the key, understanding scale, identifying features, and orienting with a compass rose
The student can describe what soil is and why it is important for growing food
The student can describe the geography of their local area with specific detail
The student has begun geographic reasoning — can look at a place on a map and make basic observations about why people would or would not live there
The student reaches for a map when a place is mentioned — map reference is becoming a habit, not an assignment

narration_prompts.detail: How many are there? Which is the biggest? Which is the smallest? Which ones are close together? Which ones are far apart?

narration_prompts.prompt: Pick a major river and tell me about it.
narration_prompts.detail: Where is it? What does it provide? Why did people settle near it?
What do they do to the people who live near them? How do they affect travel and trade?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the difference between looking at the globe and looking at the flat map?
narration_prompts.detail: What does the globe show you that the flat map does not? Why do we need both?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about the geography where we live.
narration_prompts.detail: What is the terrain like? Is there water nearby? Why do you think people settled here?

narration_prompts.prompt: If you were choosing a place to build a city, what geographic features would you want?
Tell me about the Earth's plates and how they move.
narration_prompts.detail: What happens when they push together? Why does this matter?
What is it made of? Why does it matter? What happens when soil is destroyed?

narration_prompts.prompt: Explain how to read a map.
narration_prompts.detail: What is the key? What is the scale? What is a compass rose? Why do flat maps distort the Earth?

cross_connections.description: The river valley civilizations studied in 1.1.1 are the first lesson in how geography determines where civilizations arise. The student who maps the Nile, Tigris-Euphrates, Indus, and Yellow River in 1.2.1 is building the geographic foundation that makes 1.1.1's history comprehensible.

Physical geography provides the stage; climate determines what can grow and what can survive on that stage. 1.2.1's landforms and 1.2.2's climate zones work together to explain the full environmental picture. Mountains create rain shadows. Ocean currents drive weather. The landforms studied here are the foundation for climate understanding in 1.2.2.
The rivers, coastlines, and harbors studied here are the same features that create trade routes in 1.2.3. A river is simultaneously a geographic feature and an economic highway.

The survival skills in 2.4.1 connect directly to geographic understanding. The student who has found water in the wild understands viscerally why rivers matter. The student who has built shelter understands why terrain matters.
Map scales, distances between places, and the relative sizes of continents and oceans all connect to the estimation and proportion skills developed in 1.5.1.
The narrations and map labeling in this packet reinforce the writing and expression skills developed in 3.2.1.

content.why_this_matters:
Look around you. Whatever you see — your house, a road, a river, a hill, a flat field stretching to the horizon — that is geography. Geography is the shape of the world. It is the mountains and valleys, the oceans and rivers, the deserts and forests that make up the surface of the planet you live on.

Geography matters because it decides almost everything about how people live. Where cities get built. Where farms can grow food. Where roads go. Where armies march. Where ships can sail. The shape of the land is the first fact about any place, and every other fact follows from it.

You are going to learn the basic shape of the world — what it looks like, where things are, and why the shape of the land matters so much. You will work with maps constantly, because maps are the language of geography. A person who can read a map understands the world in a way that a person who cannot read a map simply does not.

This is not about memorizing names on a map and forgetting them. This is about building a picture of the world in your mind — a picture so real that when someone mentions the Himalayas or the Amazon River or the Sahara Desert, you can see where it is, what it looks like, and begin to understand what life there must be like. And you will learn that the world is not finished — the ground beneath your feet is moving, mountains are still being built, and the map you see today is just a snapshot of a planet that is always changing.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Basic spatial awareness** — the student knows where things are on a globe and a map, and understands that maps represent real, physical places
2. **Continental and oceanic literacy** — the student can identify and locate the seven continents and five oceans without hesitation
3. **Major landform recognition** — mountains, rivers, deserts, plains, coastlines, valleys, plateaus, and islands as real features with real effects on human life
4. **A felt sense that the Earth is dynamic** — mountains grow, continents move, rivers change course, and none of this is finished
5. **Cardinal direction competence** — north, south, east, west as tools for orientation, not just vocabulary words
6. **Map reading as a habit** — the student reaches for a map when a place is mentioned, not as an assignment but as a reflex
7. **Map literacy** — the ability to read a map, understand a key, interpret symbols, orient using a compass rose, and understand scale
8. **Soil as a living thing** — understanding that dirt is not just dirt, but the foundation of all food production
9. **The beginning of geographic reasoning** — the student starts to connect physical features to human activity: "People live near that river because they need water"
content.core_content:
### Part 1: The Earth Is Round — And That Changes Everything

The Earth is a sphere — a ball. Not a perfect ball (it bulges slightly at the middle), but close enough. This is the first and most important geographic fact.

When you look at a flat map on a wall, you are looking at someone's attempt to flatten a ball onto a flat surface. This cannot be done perfectly. Every flat map distorts something — it stretches some areas, shrinks others, bends some shapes. This is not a flaw in the map. It is a consequence of trying to represent a round thing on a flat surface. It is physically impossible to do this without distortion.

This is why you need both a globe and a flat map. The globe shows you the real shape and the real relationships between places. The flat map lets you see everything at once and is easier to work with for details. Each tool has strengths and weaknesses.

**Activity:** Hold the globe. Find where you live. Now find the same place on the wall map. They look the same, but notice the areas near the top and bottom of the globe — the Arctic and Antarctic. On the globe, they are relatively small areas. On most flat maps, they are stretched enormously wide. Greenland on a standard flat map looks as big as Africa. On a globe, you can see that Africa is actually fourteen times larger than Greenland. The map lied — not on purpose, but because of the distortion problem.

### Part 2: The Earth Is Not Finished

Most people think of the ground as solid and permanent. It is neither. The Earth beneath your feet is moving right now — slowly, but constantly. The continents you see on a map have not always been where they are. Two hundred million years ago, all the land on Earth was joined in one giant supercontinent that scientists call Pangaea. It broke apart. The pieces drifted. They are still drifting.

This is not a theory someone made up. The evidence is overwhelming. The coastline of South America fits against the coastline of Africa like puzzle pieces. The same fossils appear on both sides of the Atlantic Ocean — animals that could not have swum across. The same rock formations appear on continents now separated by thousands of miles of water. The Earth moved, and the evidence is written in stone.

**What makes mountains:** Mountains are not just tall things that have always been there. They are built by enormous forces. When two tectonic plates push into each other, the crust crumples upward. The Himalayas are still growing because the plate carrying India is still pushing into the plate carrying Asia. Mount Everest grows roughly 4 millimeters per year. That does not sound like much, but over millions of years, it built the roof of the world. Some mountains are volcanic — molten rock from deep inside the Earth pushes up through the crust, building a mountain from the inside out. Mount Fuji, Mount Kilimanjaro, and Mount St. Helens (which erupted in 1980) are volcanic mountains.

**What this means for you:** The world map you see today is a snapshot. It will look different in a hundred million years. Geography is not fixed. It is a process.

### Part 3: Land and Water — The Basic Division

The surface of the Earth is divided into two things: land and water. This sounds simple, but the proportions are surprising and important.

**About 71% of the Earth's surface is water.** Nearly three-quarters. If you were an alien approaching Earth from space, the first thing you would notice is that this is a water planet. The oceans dominate.

**About 29% of the Earth's surface is land.** Less than a third. And the land is not evenly distributed — most of it is concentrated in the Northern Hemisphere (the top half of the globe). The Southern Hemisphere is mostly ocean.

**The Five Oceans:**
1. **Pacific Ocean** — The largest. It is larger than all the land on Earth combined. It stretches from Asia and Australia on one side to the Americas on the other. It is enormous. Looking at a globe centered on the Pacific, you can see a hemisphere that is almost entirely water.
2. **Atlantic Ocean** — The second largest. It separates the Americas from Europe and Africa. It is the ocean that European explorers crossed to reach the Americas — a journey that took weeks under sail.
3. **Indian Ocean** — The third largest. It lies between Africa, Asia, and Australia. It has been one of the world's busiest trade routes for thousands of years.
4. **Southern Ocean (Antarctic Ocean)** — The ocean surrounding Antarctica. Cold, rough, and dangerous. It circles the bottom of the world.
5. **Arctic Ocean** — The smallest ocean. It sits at the top of the world, around the North Pole. Much of it is covered in ice, though the amount of ice is changing.

**Activity:** On the globe, find each ocean. Notice how they connect — the oceans are not truly separate. They are all one continuous body of water that wraps around the continents. Now look at the wall map and find the same oceans. Notice how the flat map makes the Arctic and Southern oceans look different than they do on the globe.

### Part 4: The Seven Continents

The land is divided into seven continents — large, continuous masses of land. (Some geographers count differently — combining Europe and Asia into Eurasia, for example — but seven is the standard count used in most atlases.)

**1. Asia** — The largest continent. It stretches from Turkey and the Middle East in the west to Japan and the Philippines in the east, and from Siberia in the north to Indonesia in the south. More people live in Asia than on all the other continents combined. It contains the highest mountains on Earth (the Himalayas), the largest country by area (Russia, which stretches across both Asia and Europe), and two of the most populous countries (China and India).

**2. Africa** — The second-largest continent. It is much larger than it appears on most flat maps. Africa contains the Sahara Desert (the largest hot desert in the world), the Nile River (the longest river in the world, or close to it — the Amazon competes for the title), vast tropical rainforests, enormous savannas, and the Great Rift Valley. It is the continent where human beings first evolved.

**3. North America** — The third-largest continent. It stretches from the Arctic reaches of Canada to the tropical forests of Central America. It contains the Rocky Mountains, the Great Plains, the Mississippi River system, the Great Lakes, and enormous stretches of farmland. The United States, Canada, and Mexico are the three largest countries.

**4. South America** — Contains the Amazon River (the largest river in the world by volume of water — it carries more water than the next seven largest rivers combined) and the Amazon Rainforest (the largest tropical forest on Earth). The Andes Mountains run the entire length of the continent's western coast — the longest mountain range in the world. The continent narrows to a point at its southern tip, where Patagonia faces the fierce winds of the Southern Ocean.

**5. Antarctica** — The coldest, driest, windiest continent. It sits at the South Pole, almost entirely covered by a massive ice sheet that is up to three miles thick in places. No country owns Antarctica. No one lives there permanently. It is the only continent with no native human population. Scientists from many countries maintain research stations there.

**6. Europe** — Physically, Europe is a large peninsula extending westward from Asia. (A peninsula is a piece of land that sticks out into water, surrounded by water on three sides.) It is the second-smallest continent, but it has had an outsized influence on world history because of its geography: long coastlines, many natural harbors, navigable rivers, and a temperate climate that supports agriculture.

**7. Australia (Oceania)** — The smallest continent. Australia is also a country — the only continent that is a single country. It is largely dry and flat, with a vast interior desert (the Outback) and a green, populated coastal fringe. Oceania includes the thousands of islands scattered across the Pacific Ocean — Polynesia, Melanesia, and Micronesia.

**Activity:** On the globe, find each continent. Touch each one. Say its name. Notice which ones are close together (Africa and Europe are separated by only a narrow strait at Gibraltar; Asia and North America nearly touch at the Bering Strait in the far north). Notice which ones are far apart (Australia is isolated by ocean on all sides). These distances and proximities matter enormously — they determine who trades with whom, who fights whom, and who knows about whom.

### Part 5: Mountains — The Walls of the World

Mountains are raised areas of land — dramatically higher than the land around them. They are formed over millions of years by forces deep inside the Earth pushing rock upward. They are some of the most important features on the planet because they act as walls.

Mountains block movement. Armies struggle to cross them. Trade caravans must find passes through them. Rain falls on one side and not the other. People on one side of a mountain range often develop differently from people on the other side — different languages, different customs, different governments — because the mountains separate them.

Old mountains are shorter than young mountains. The Appalachian Mountains in eastern North America were once as tall as the Himalayas — hundreds of millions of years ago. They have been worn down by wind, water, and ice ever since. The Himalayas are young mountains; the Appalachians are old. You can tell by looking.

**Major Mountain Ranges to Know:**

- **The Himalayas** — The highest mountains on Earth. They separate the Indian subcontinent from the rest of Asia. Mount Everest, the highest point on Earth, is in the Himalayas. These mountains are so high and so wide that they have separated India and China for most of history — two enormous civilizations that developed largely independently because an impassable wall of rock stood between them.

- **The Alps** — A mountain range in Europe that separates Italy from the rest of the continent. Throughout history, anyone wanting to invade Italy from the north had to find a way through or around the Alps. Hannibal famously crossed them with elephants. Napoleon crossed them with an army. Both were considered extraordinary feats because the Alps are extraordinarily difficult to cross.

- **The Andes** — The longest mountain range in the world, running the entire western length of South America. The Inca Empire was built in these mountains. People still live at very high altitudes in the Andes, farming on terraced mountainsides.

- **The Rocky Mountains** — Running through western North America from Canada to New Mexico. They were a major barrier to westward expansion in the United States. Early settlers had to find passes through the Rockies, and the difficulty of crossing them shaped the entire history of the American West.

- **The Ural Mountains** — Running north-south through Russia. They are traditionally considered the boundary between Europe and Asia, though they are not very high or difficult to cross. Geography does not always provide clean boundaries.

- **The Atlas Mountains** — In northwestern Africa. They separate the Mediterranean coast from the Sahara Desert.

**Activity:** Find each mountain range on the globe and the wall map. Trace them with your finger. Notice how they run — some run east-west (the Himalayas, the Alps), some run north-south (the Andes, the Rockies, the Urals). The direction a mountain range runs matters because it determines what it blocks and what it allows to pass.

### Part 6: Rivers — The Roads Before Roads

Before humans built roads, rivers were the primary way to move goods and people. A river is a natural highway. It carries water downhill from high ground to the sea, and anything floating on it moves with the current.

Rivers are important for three reasons:

**1. Water supply.** Every human being needs fresh water to survive. Rivers provide it. This is why nearly every major city in the world is built on a river.

**2. Fertile soil.** Rivers that flood deposit rich soil on their banks. This soil grows crops. The first civilizations — as you learn in 1.1.1 — all formed in river valleys because the soil near rivers was the best farmland available.

**3. Transportation.** Rivers move things. Before trains and trucks, rivers were how heavy goods traveled. Logs floated downstream. Boats carried grain, cloth, metal, and people. A city on a river could trade with every other city on the same river.

**The water cycle** connects all water on Earth. Water evaporates from oceans and lakes, rises into the atmosphere, forms clouds, falls as rain or snow, runs into streams and rivers, flows back to the ocean, and starts again. No new water is being created. The water you drink today is the same water the dinosaurs drank. It has been cycling through the Earth system for billions of years.

**Groundwater** is water stored underground in rock and soil. When you dig a well, you are tapping into groundwater. Much of the world's fresh water is underground, not on the surface. The Ogallala Aquifer beneath the Great Plains of the United States holds enough water to cover all 50 states in a foot and a half of water — and it is being drained faster than it is being refilled. This matters enormously for the future.

**Major Rivers to Know:**

- **The Nile** — Flows north through northeastern Africa to the Mediterranean Sea. The longest river in Africa and one of the longest in the world. Ancient Egyptian civilization was built entirely on the Nile's annual flood cycle.

- **The Amazon** — The largest river in the world by volume. It carries about one-fifth of all the river water on Earth. Its basin — the area it drains — is the Amazon Rainforest, the largest tropical forest on the planet.

- **The Mississippi-Missouri River System** — Drains most of the central United States. The Mississippi flows from Minnesota to the Gulf of Mexico. With its tributaries, it creates a vast transportation network across the American interior. New Orleans sits where the Mississippi meets the sea — and controlling that point has been strategically vital for centuries.

- **The Tigris and Euphrates** — Flow through modern Iraq. Mesopotamia — "the land between the rivers" — was the birthplace of some of the earliest civilizations. You study this in 1.1.1.

- **The Yangtze and Yellow (Huang He) Rivers** — The two great rivers of China. The Yangtze is the longest river in Asia. The Yellow River is called "China's Sorrow" because its floods have killed more people throughout history than any other natural feature on Earth. Chinese civilization developed along these rivers.

- **The Ganges** — Flows through India. Sacred to Hindus. One of the most important rivers in human history, supporting one of the densest populations on Earth.

- **The Danube** — Flows through central and eastern Europe, passing through or bordering ten countries. It was the northern border of the Roman Empire for centuries.

- **The Rhine** — Flows through western Europe. Together with the Danube, it formed the natural defensive boundary of the Roman Empire.

**Activity:** On a blank outline map, draw and label each of these rivers. For each one, identify: What continent is it on? What direction does it flow? Where does it empty into the sea? Are there any famous cities on it? Keep this map. You will add to it.

### Part 7: Deserts — The Empty Places

Deserts are areas that receive very little rainfall — less than 10 inches (25 centimeters) per year. They are not always hot. Some deserts are cold. What makes a desert a desert is the lack of water.

Deserts are barriers. They are hard to cross, hard to live in, and hard to farm. Like mountains, they separate peoples and civilizations.

**Major Deserts to Know:**

- **The Sahara** — The largest hot desert in the world, covering most of northern Africa. It is roughly the size of the entire United States. The Sahara separates North Africa (which has historically been connected to the Mediterranean world) from sub-Saharan Africa. Crossing the Sahara required camels, knowledge of water sources, and extraordinary endurance. Trade routes across the Sahara connected West African gold and salt to North African and Mediterranean markets.

- **The Arabian Desert** — Covers most of the Arabian Peninsula. Beneath its sands lie some of the largest oil reserves on Earth — a fact that has made this desert one of the most strategically important places in the modern world.

- **The Gobi Desert** — In Mongolia and northern China. The Silk Road passed along its edges. Genghis Khan and the Mongols emerged from the steppe lands near the Gobi.

- **The Kalahari and Namib Deserts** — In southern Africa. The Namib is one of the oldest deserts on Earth.

- **The Australian Outback** — The vast, dry interior of Australia. Aboriginal Australians have lived in this environment for over 50,000 years — the longest continuous habitation of any landscape on Earth.

**Activity:** Find each desert on the map. Color them on your outline map (use yellow or tan). Notice how deserts cluster — there is a band of desert running across northern Africa, through the Middle East, and into central Asia. This is not a coincidence. The geography of wind and rain patterns creates this desert belt.

### Part 8: Plains, Valleys & Plateaus — The Flat Spaces

Plains are large, flat (or gently rolling) areas of land. They are the opposite of mountains — easy to cross, easy to farm (if there is enough water), and easy to build on. But they are also easy to invade. A civilization built on a flat plain has no natural walls to protect it.

**Valleys** are low areas between mountains or hills, often carved by rivers. Valleys concentrate water, create fertile soil, and provide natural pathways for travel. The great river valley civilizations you study in 1.1.1 — Mesopotamia, Egypt, the Indus Valley, China — all formed in valleys where rivers deposited rich soil.

**Plateaus** are flat areas at high elevation — like a table lifted into the sky. The Tibetan Plateau, the highest and largest plateau on Earth, sits at an average elevation of 4,500 meters. The air is thin. Farming is difficult. But the plateau controls the water supply for much of Asia — rivers that feed billions of people begin as snowmelt on the Tibetan Plateau.

**Important Plains:**

- **The Great Plains** — The vast interior of North America, stretching from Canada to Texas. Some of the most productive farmland on Earth. Before European settlement, this was the domain of the bison and the indigenous peoples who depended on them.

- **The North European Plain** — A flat stretch running from France through Germany, Poland, and into Russia. Because it has no natural barriers, it has been the invasion route for every major European war. Armies can march across it in any direction. This geographic fact explains much of European history.

- **The Russian Steppe** — The enormous grasslands stretching across Russia and Central Asia. Horse-riding nomadic peoples — the Scythians, the Huns, the Mongols — used these grasslands as highways, riding across them to build the largest land empires in history.

- **The Indo-Gangetic Plain** — The flat, fertile plain in northern India formed by the Ganges and Indus rivers. One of the most densely populated areas on Earth, and the agricultural heartland of the Indian subcontinent.

**Key Insight:** Plains are good for farming and building. But plains without natural borders are vulnerable. The most fought-over places in history tend to be fertile plains with no mountains or rivers to protect them. You will explore this pattern much more deeply in 1.2.4.

### Part 9: Soil — The Skin of the Earth

Soil seems boring until you realize that all food comes from it. Every plant grows in soil. Every animal that eats plants depends on soil. Every animal that eats those animals depends on soil. You depend on soil. If the soil dies, everything above it dies.

**How soil forms:** Rock breaks down over thousands of years through weathering — wind, water, ice, and chemical reactions slowly crumble rock into smaller and smaller pieces. Plants grow in the cracks, their roots breaking rock further. Dead plants and animals decompose and mix with the mineral fragments. The result is soil: a living mixture of minerals, organic matter, water, air, and billions of organisms — bacteria, fungi, worms, insects — that make the whole system work.

**Not all soil is the same.** The black soil of Ukraine and the American Midwest is among the richest on Earth — deep, fertile, full of organic matter. This is why these regions became the breadbaskets of the world. The thin, sandy soil of the Sahara's edge grows almost nothing. The red laterite soil of the tropics looks dramatic but is often nutrient-poor because heavy rains wash nutrients away. Civilizations rise where the soil is good and struggle where it is not.

**Soil can be destroyed.** Poor farming practices — plowing too much, not rotating crops, removing all plant cover — can exhaust soil in a generation. The Dust Bowl of the 1930s in the American Great Plains happened because farmers had stripped the native grasses from millions of acres of prairie. When drought came, the exposed soil simply blew away in massive dust storms that blackened the sky. The lesson: soil is not permanent. It can be created over millennia and destroyed in decades.

### Part 10: Coastlines and Islands — Where Land Meets Water

The places where land meets the ocean are some of the most important geographic features on Earth. Coastlines determine who can access the sea — and access to the sea has been one of the greatest advantages in all of human history.

**Why coasts matter:**
- **Trade.** Ships can carry far more cargo than any land vehicle. A country with a coastline can trade by sea. A country without a coastline (landlocked) is at an enormous economic disadvantage.
- **Food.** The ocean is full of fish. Coastal peoples have an additional food source that inland peoples do not.
- **Defense.** The sea is a natural moat. Island nations — like Britain and Japan — are protected by the water around them in a way that continental nations are not.
- **Harbors.** Not all coastline is equal. A natural harbor — a sheltered indentation where ships can anchor safely — is one of the most valuable geographic features on Earth. Many of the world's greatest cities exist because of their harbors: New York, London, Istanbul, Hong Kong, Sydney, San Francisco.

**How islands form:** Some islands are volcanic — they rose from the ocean floor. Hawaii, Iceland, and the Galapagos Islands were built by volcanoes. Other islands are pieces of continent separated by rising sea levels — Britain was once connected to mainland Europe by a land bridge. Japan was once connected to mainland Asia. Islands create isolation, which creates unique cultures, unique species, and unique strategic positions. An island is naturally defended. You must cross water to reach it.

**Peninsulas** are land almost surrounded by water — connected to the mainland by a narrow strip. Italy, Korea, Florida, India (the subcontinent), and Scandinavia are all peninsulas or near-peninsulas. Peninsulas have enormous coastlines relative to their area, which means access to the sea from many directions.

**Islands to Notice:**
- **The British Isles** — Britain's island position has shaped its entire history. It has not been successfully invaded since 1066. Its navy became the most powerful in the world partly because, as an island nation, naval power was a survival requirement.
- **Japan** — Another island nation whose geography shaped its history. Japan was isolated enough to develop a unique culture, but close enough to the Asian mainland to benefit from Chinese and Korean cultural influence.
- **Madagascar** — A large island off the southeast coast of Africa. Separated from the mainland long enough for unique species to evolve there that exist nowhere else on Earth.
- **The Indonesian and Philippine Archipelagos** — Chains of thousands of islands in Southeast Asia. These islands sit astride some of the most important sea lanes in the world.

**Activity:** On your map, trace the coastlines of the continents. Find and mark five natural harbors. Find and mark the islands listed above. Notice: which continents have long, complex coastlines with many harbors? (Europe.) Which have smoother coastlines with fewer harbors? (Africa.) This difference in coastline has had enormous consequences for history.

### Part 11: How to Read a Map

A map is a tool. Like any tool, you must learn how to use it.

**Orientation:** Most maps put north at the top, but this is a convention, not a law of nature. Check the compass rose — the symbol that shows directions. Know which way is north, south, east, and west. On a globe, north is toward the North Pole, south is toward the South Pole.

**The key (or legend):** Every map has symbols — lines for roads, dots for cities, colors for elevations or biomes. The key tells you what each symbol means. Always read the key first. A map without a key is a picture, not a tool.

**Scale:** A map is smaller than the real world. The scale tells you how much smaller. A scale might say "1 inch = 100 miles." That means every inch on the map represents 100 miles of real distance. Understanding scale is understanding that the map is shrinking everything by the same amount, so the relationships are preserved even though the size is not.

**Contour lines and elevation:** Some maps use contour lines to show elevation — each line represents a specific height above sea level. Lines close together mean steep terrain. Lines far apart mean flat terrain. Learning to read contour lines is learning to see the shape of the land from a flat piece of paper.

**The hemispheres:** The equator divides the Earth into Northern and Southern Hemispheres. Most of the world's land is in the Northern Hemisphere. Most of the world's people live in the Northern Hemisphere. The Southern Hemisphere is mostly ocean. The equator receives the most direct sunlight. The tropics — the band between the Tropic of Cancer and the Tropic of Capricorn — are the warmest regions on Earth. This matters enormously for climate, agriculture, and where people live.

### Part 12: Putting It Together — Reading the Land

Everything you have learned in this packet is connected. Mountains, rivers, deserts, plains, coastlines, soil, and islands are not separate categories to memorize. They are features of a single, interconnected physical world. The skill you are building is the ability to look at a place — on a map or in real life — and understand what the geography means for the people who live there.

**Practice this thinking:**

Look at Egypt on the map. What do you see? A river (the Nile) running through a desert (the Sahara). The desert protects Egypt on both sides. The river provides water and fertile soil in a narrow strip. This is why Egyptian civilization formed where it did and lasted as long as it did — the geography was ideal for a civilization and terrible for anyone trying to invade it.

Look at Greece on the map. What do you see? Mountains everywhere, dividing the land into small valleys, surrounded by sea on three sides, with many islands. This geography explains why Greece was never a single unified country in the ancient world — the mountains separated people into small, independent city-states. And the sea made the Greeks into sailors and traders.

Look at the central United States. What do you see? The Mississippi River system draining an enormous flat plain. This geography explains why the interior of North America became the most productive agricultural region on Earth — flat, fertile land with a natural transportation network built in.

**This is geographic reasoning.** You look at the land and you understand what it means. This skill will grow over years. Right now, you are building the foundation — learning the shapes, the names, the locations. In Weathering Storms, you will analyze how geography shaped specific historical events. In Standing Ground, you will use geography to understand modern strategic reality. But it all starts here, with the simple act of looking at a map and seeing what the land tells you.

### Part 13: Your Local Geography

Geography is not only about distant places. It starts where you are.

**Observe and map your local area:**
- What is the terrain where you live? Flat? Hilly? Mountainous? Coastal?
- Is there a river or body of water nearby? What is it called? Where does it come from? Where does it go?
- What direction is north from your house? (Use a compass or the sun — the sun rises in the east and sets in the west.)
- What is the highest point you can see from where you live? What is the lowest?
- Why do you think people settled where you live? What geographic advantages does your location have?
- What is the soil like? Is it dark or light? Sandy or sticky? Does it crumble or clump?

**Activity:** Draw a map of your neighborhood or town. Include the major features: roads, rivers or streams, hills, any notable landmarks. Mark north. This is your first original map. Compare it to a real map of your area (use an atlas or a printed map). What did you get right? What did you miss? What does the real map show you that you had not noticed?
content.formative_practices:
*Geography at Stretching Branches is learned through maps, observation, and conversation. The following studies are worked through over the course of this packet.*

### Study 1: Daily Map Reference [Ongoing — Backbone of the Packet]
Every time a place is mentioned in any subject — history, science, reading, family conversation, news — the student finds it on the wall map or globe. This is not an assignment; it is a habit. The goal is that reaching for a map becomes automatic. The mentor models this behavior: "We just read about the Amazon. Let's find it." Over weeks, the student begins doing this unprompted.

### Study 2: Globe Exploration [First Weeks — Ongoing]
Spend time with the globe. Spin it. Find the continents. Find the oceans. Trace the equator with your finger. Find where you live. Find the biggest ocean. Find the biggest continent. Find the smallest. Notice how much of the Earth is water. The globe should be accessible throughout the packet for reference and exploration.

### Study 3: Continent and Ocean Mastery [First Weeks]
Using the globe, wall map, and atlas, the student learns to identify and locate all seven continents and five oceans. This is practiced through pointing, labeling blank maps, and casual quizzing during other activities. The goal is automatic recognition — the student sees a continent shape and knows what it is without thinking, the same way they recognize letters.

### Study 4: River and Mountain Map Project [Early-to-Mid Packet]
On a large blank world map (or several regional maps), the student draws and labels the major rivers and mountain ranges listed in this packet. The map is colored and displayed. Each feature is discussed: What does this river provide? What does this mountain range separate? This map stays visible and is referenced throughout the packet.

### Study 5: Soil Investigation [Mid-Packet — Hands-On]
Go outside. Dig up some soil. Look at it carefully. Is it dark or light? Sandy or sticky? Does it have worms or insects? Does it crumble or clump? Compare soil from different locations — a garden bed, a forest floor, a sandy area, a clay bank. Different soils look, feel, and behave differently. The student should begin to see soil as a living system, not just dirt.

### Study 6: Atlas Exploration [Ongoing]
Regular sessions (at least weekly) of browsing the atlas together. The mentor and student pick a region, look at its physical features, and talk about what they see. "Look at Italy — it's shaped like a boot. Mountains across the top, sea all around. Where would you build a city?" This unstructured exploration builds geographic curiosity and spatial familiarity.

### Study 7: Local Geography Walk [Mid-Packet]
The student and mentor walk their local area with geographic eyes. They observe terrain, water features, the direction of slopes, where buildings are concentrated, and why. The student draws a map afterward. This grounds abstract geographic knowledge in physical experience.

### Study 8: Globe vs. Map Comparison [Mid-Packet]
The student compares the globe to the flat wall map, looking for distortions. Which places look bigger on the flat map than they really are? Which look smaller? Why does this happen? This study builds critical thinking about representations — maps are tools that show some things well and others poorly.

### Study 9: Landform Scavenger Hunt [Ongoing]
Using books, maps, or Google Earth, find and name examples of: a mountain range, a river valley, a plain, a plateau, an island, a peninsula, and a coastline. For each one, the student describes what they see and explains what makes it that type of landform. If you live near any of these features, visit them.

### Study 10: Desert and Climate Zones [Late Packet]
Using the atlas, the student identifies the world's major deserts and notices the pattern: a band of desert at roughly the same latitudes. The student also notices that tropical areas are near the equator and cold areas are near the poles. This is preparation for 1.2.2's deeper study of climate.

### Study 11: Build a Landform [Hands-On — Any Time]
Using clay, sand, or papier-mache, build a physical model of a landscape that includes at least a mountain, a valley, a river, a plain, and a coastline. Label the features. Pour water on the mountain and watch where it flows — this is how rivers form. This tactile experience makes abstract geography concrete.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Continental and Oceanic Identification
**Developing well:** The student can find all seven continents and five oceans on both a globe and a flat map without hesitation. They know which continents are connected and which are separated by ocean. They have a sense of relative size.

**Developing slowly:** The student confuses continents, cannot consistently locate more than two or three, or treats the exercise as a memorization chore rather than spatial knowledge.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the wall map visible and referenced daily? Has the student had enough hands-on time with the globe? Continent recognition comes from repeated, casual exposure — not from drilling sessions.

### 2. Landform Recognition and Vocabulary
**Developing well:** The student uses geographic terms naturally — "that's a valley," "this is a peninsula" — and can explain basic formation processes. They point out landforms in the real world or in images without prompting. They can locate major rivers, mountain ranges, and deserts on a map and name specific features, saying something about why each matters: "The Himalayas separate India from China." "The Nile floods and makes the soil good for farming."

**Developing slowly:** The student cannot name basic landforms or confuses terms. Rivers, mountains, and deserts blur together. Geographic vocabulary does not stick despite repeated exposure.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you using geographic terms yourself in conversation? Children absorb vocabulary from context. If you point to hills and say "look at those hills" instead of "look at that ridge," you are missing an opportunity. Also: are you connecting terms to real or visual examples? Abstract definitions do not stick at this age — concrete examples do. Did the student draw and label these features on a blank map? Physical drawing builds spatial memory far more effectively than looking and listening.

### 3. Map and Globe Literacy
**Developing well:** The student can orient themselves on a globe — find their continent, their country, the equator, the major oceans. They can read a simple map key and use scale to estimate distance. They understand that flat maps distort the Earth. They know north, south, east, west and can orient on a map, using cardinal directions in conversation: "Africa is south of Europe." "The Rocky Mountains are in the western part of North America."

**Developing slowly:** The student cannot find basic features on a globe. Map keys and scale remain confusing. The concept of projection distortion does not register. The student cannot consistently identify cardinal directions on a map.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the student had enough hands-on time with the globe? Map literacy is a skill that requires practice, not just instruction. The student needs to use maps and globes repeatedly, in different contexts, over weeks. One lesson is not enough. Have you practiced with a compass? Have you identified north from your house using the sun? Physical, embodied orientation — standing outside and pointing north — often clarifies what map-based instruction does not.

### 4. Water System Understanding
**Developing well:** The student understands that rivers flow from high to low, that they carry water to the ocean, that the water cycle is continuous, and that fresh water is a limited resource. They can trace a river on a map and explain where the water goes.

**Developing slowly:** Water systems remain abstract. The student cannot explain why rivers flow in one direction or how rain connects to rivers.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you done the hands-on water activities? Pouring water over a clay model and watching it flow downhill is worth more than any explanation. Have you traced a real local water source — where does your tap water come from? Following water from tap to reservoir to river to rain makes the cycle concrete.

### 5. Soil Awareness
**Developing well:** The student understands that soil is a living system, not just dirt. They can describe what soil is made of and why it matters for growing food. They have handled different types of soil and noticed differences.

**Developing slowly:** Soil remains "just dirt." The student has no sense of why soil quality matters or how soil forms.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you done the soil investigation? Children who dig in actual soil and examine what they find almost always become interested. The problem is usually that the activity was skipped, not that the student is unresponsive to it.

### 6. Map Habit Formation
**Developing well:** The student reaches for a map or globe when a place is mentioned. This behavior is becoming automatic. The student may ask unprompted, "Where is that on the map?"

**Developing slowly:** The student only looks at maps when directed to. Map reference feels like an assignment rather than a habit.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you modeling the behavior? If you reach for the map every time a place comes up, the student will imitate. If you only use maps during geography sessions, the student will treat maps as geography tools rather than general-purpose tools.

### 7. Beginning Geographic Reasoning
**Developing well:** The student can look at a place on the map and make basic observations about human life there: "There's a river, so people could farm." "It's an island, so they would need boats." "The mountains would make it hard to get through." These observations need not be sophisticated — they just need to be present.

**Developing slowly:** The student sees maps as pictures rather than information. They can identify features but do not connect features to human activity.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you asking geographic reasoning questions during map sessions? "Why do you think a city grew here?" "What would be hard about living there?" These questions model the reasoning you want the student to develop.

### 8. Geographic Curiosity
**Developing well:** The student asks geographic questions unprompted — "Why is that river there?" "What's on the other side of that mountain?" "How deep is the ocean?" They notice geographic features in daily life and in stories.

**Developing slowly:** Geography remains a subject to be gotten through rather than a way of seeing the world. No questions emerge. No connections are made outside of study time.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you modeling geographic curiosity yourself? When you drive past a river, do you wonder aloud where it starts? When you see a mountain, do you ask what built it? Children learn to be curious by watching curious adults.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Treating Geography as a Memorization Subject
The temptation is to turn geography into a list of facts: "The Nile is in Africa. The Amazon is in South America. The Himalayas are the highest mountains." Or: name the continents, name the oceans, name the longest rivers. Lists have their place, but they are not geography. Geography is understanding why things are where they are and what they mean for human life. A student who can recite the names but cannot explain why rivers matter, what mountains do, or how mountains form has memorized a list, not learned geography. Keep the focus on understanding processes and systems. Always connect the fact to its meaning.

### Under-Investing in Map Materials
A geography education without a quality globe, a wall map, and an atlas is like a music education without an instrument. These are not optional supplements. They are the core learning tools. Invest in a good globe (not a tiny decorative one — a full-size educational globe), a large wall map, and a well-designed children's atlas. These will serve for years.

### Doing Geography Only During Geography Time
If geography is a forty-minute block on Tuesday and Thursday, the student will learn geography as a subject. If geography is the habit of locating every place mentioned in any context, the student will develop geographic literacy. The latter is what this packet aims to build. It requires the mentor to make geographic connections constantly — not as extra work but as a way of engaging with the world.

### Staying Indoors
Geography is an outdoor subject trapped in an indoor world. Every time you can take the student outside to look at real terrain, real water, real soil, real sky — do it. A walk along a creek teaches more about water systems than a chapter in a book. A hike up a hill teaches more about elevation than a contour map. The local geography walk in the student packet is not optional enrichment — it is a core activity. Go outside and look at the land. Use books for framework and structure. Use the world for evidence and experience.

### Neglecting the Globe for Flat Maps
Flat maps are convenient. They are also liars. Every flat map distorts the Earth. The student who only sees flat maps develops a distorted picture of the world — Greenland too big, Africa too small, the Pacific Ocean not vast enough. The globe corrects all of this. Use it constantly.

### Skipping Soil
Soil is the least dramatic topic in this packet and the most important. Mountains are exciting. Oceans are vast. Soil is... dirt. But soil is the foundation of all food production, and therefore the foundation of all civilization. Do not skip the soil activities. The Dust Bowl lesson in particular — soil destruction through human misuse — is one of the most important geographic lessons in the entire curriculum.

### Moving Too Fast Through Map Skills
Map reading is a skill that develops through practice, not through a single lesson. Spread map work throughout the entire packet. Return to it again and again. The goal is not "the student passed a map quiz" but "the student reaches for a map when they want to understand something."

### Rushing to Cover Everything
The student packet covers a lot of territory (literally). Not every feature needs to be mastered in a single pass. It is better to know five rivers deeply — where they are, what they provide, who lived along them — than to have heard of twenty rivers and remember none. Prioritize depth over breadth for the youngest students. Older Stretching Branches students can cover more ground.
mentor_guide.narration_evaluation:
### Prompt 1: The Continents
**Strong:** Names all seven continents, locates them accurately relative to each other, and makes at least one interesting observation about size, proximity, or isolation. May compare continents to each other.
**Weak:** Can name some continents but cannot locate them or describe their relationships. No sense of relative position or size.

### Prompt 2: Plate Tectonics
**Strong:** Describes the movement of plates, the building of mountains through collision, and gives a specific example (Himalayas, Pangaea). May express wonder at the scale of time involved.
**Weak:** "The ground moves." No specifics, no process, no examples.

### Prompt 3: A Landform
**Strong:** Chooses a specific landform, explains how it forms, names a real example, and connects it to human life. "The Himalayas are collision mountains — India pushed into Asia and the land crumpled up. They're still growing. They're so tall that they block weather and separate India from China."
**Weak:** "Mountains are tall." No formation process, no specific example, no connection to people.

### Prompt 4: A Major River
**Strong:** Identifies a specific river, locates it on a continent, traces where it begins, what it passes through, where it ends. Describes what it provides (water, soil, transportation) and connects it to human settlement. May reference a civilization studied in 1.1.1. Explains why cities form along rivers.
**Weak:** "Rivers go to the ocean" or "Rivers have water." No specific river, no specific detail, no connection to human life.

### Prompt 5: Mountains
**Strong:** Describes mountains as barriers that affect movement, trade, and cultural development. Names at least one specific mountain range and explains what it separates or protects.
**Weak:** "Mountains are tall." No understanding of mountains as geographic forces.

### Prompt 6: Soil
**Strong:** Describes soil as a living mixture of minerals, organic matter, and organisms. Explains why it matters for food. May describe the Dust Bowl or another example of soil destruction.
**Weak:** "Soil is dirt that plants grow in." No depth, no understanding of soil as a system.

### Prompt 7: Globe vs. Map
**Strong:** Can describe at least one specific distortion — Greenland's apparent size, the stretching of polar regions — and articulate why the distortion happens (you cannot flatten a ball perfectly). Understands that both tools are useful in different ways. Can explain the key, scale, and compass rose in practical terms. May demonstrate by reading a real map.
**Weak:** Does not perceive any difference between the globe and the flat map, or cannot articulate what the difference means. Cannot explain what a map key is for or how scale works.

### Prompt 8: The Ocean
**Strong:** Describes the ocean's vastness (71% of Earth's surface), its role in weather and transportation, and why coastal civilizations had advantages. Specific details about at least one ocean.
**Weak:** "The ocean is big and has fish." No connection to civilization or climate.

### Prompt 9: Local Geography
**Strong:** Describes specific features of the local area — a nearby river, the terrain, the direction of slopes. May speculate about why people settled there. Shows that the student is observing the real world with geographic eyes.
**Weak:** Vague or generic. Cannot describe the geography of the place where they actually live.

### Prompt 10: City Placement
**Strong:** Names multiple geographic features they would want (river, harbor, flat land for farming, defensible position) and explains why each matters. This is geographic reasoning at its most basic — connecting physical features to human needs.
**Weak:** "Somewhere nice." No specific geographic reasoning.
